International Programs Diploma programs to help International students find meaningful work. --> Student Login Webinars Transcript Requests Contact Browse Programs Browse Programs Robertson College > Blog > Career Advice > Top 12 Highest-Paying Jobs In Canada Without A Degree Top 12 Highest-Paying Jobs In Canada Without A Degree By Matthew Lau Matthew Lau is a marketing expert with over 10 years of experience, specializing in integrated marketing, project management, and strategic planning. He has significantly enhanced digital engagement and conversion rates across the education, retail, and media industries. Matthew has also provided marketing consultancy services, assisting businesses in developing and implementing effective digital marketing and eCommerce strategies, creating compelling content, optimizing their online presence, and achieving substantial growth. March 3, 2025 13 min SHARE In This Article At A Glance 12 Highest-Paying Jobs In Canada Start Levelling Up Your Career Today With Robertson College Earning a degree is one way to go about pursuing a meaningful and lucrative career, but not all rewarding or well-paying jobs begin on a university campus. In fact, depending on the career path you hope to follow, enrolling in a college diploma program or seeking other specialized training opportunities may actually be the key to putting yourself ahead professionally. From firefighters and airline pilots to electricians and executive assistants, a wide variety of professionals across a spectrum of different industries have gotten where they are without once attending a university lecture. If you’re wondering how you can do the same, then this post is for you. Below, we’ve provided an up-to-date list of the top 12 highest-paying jobs in Canada that can be obtained without a degree. For more information about how Robertson College’s diploma programs can help you earn professional skills and enter the workforce within one year or less, contact one of our supportive and highly skilled Student Advisors today! All median salary statistics below are based on information provided by Indeed Canada . All labour market projections are based on information available through the Government of Canada’s Job Bank platform. At A Glance The top 12 highest-paying jobs in Canada that do not require a university degree are: 1. Firefighter Average annual salary across Canada: $166,000 In Calgary, AB: $187,000 In Edmonton, AB: $172,000 2. Air Traffic Controller Average annual base salary across Canada: $100,000 In Calgary, AB: $92,000 In Edmonton, AB: $136,000 3. Miner Average annual base salary across Canada: $149,000 In Calgary, AB: $122,000 In Edmonton, AB: $99,000 4. Police Officer Average annual salary across Canada: $91,000 In Calgary, AB: $94,000 In Edmonton, AB: $86,000 5. Construction Manager Average annual base salary across Canada: $109,000 In Calgary, AB: $116,000 In Edmonton, AB: $109,000 6. Real Estate Agent Average annual base salary across Canada: $128,000 In Calgary, AB: $116,000 In Edmonton, AB: $73,000 7. Maintenance Manager Average annual base salary across Canada: $87,000 In Calgary, AB: $74,000 In Edmonton, AB: $89,000 8. Airline Pilot Average annual base salary across Canada: $88,000 In Calgary, AB: $117,000 In Edmonton, AB: $53,000 9. Sales Manager Average annual base salary across Canada: $82,000 In Calgary, AB: $84,000 In Edmonton, AB: $91,000 10. Operations Manager Average annual base salary across Canada: $81,000 In Calgary, AB: $74,000 In Edmonton, AB: $82,000 11. Electrician Average annual base salary across Canada: $82,000 In Calgary, AB: $73,000 In Edmonton, AB: $75,000 12. Executive Assistant Average annual base salary across Canada: $63,000 In Calgary, AB: $62,000 In Edmonton, AB: $62,000 Please note that all average salaries have been rounded to the nearest thousand. 12 Highest-Paying Jobs In Canada 1. Firefighter The average annual salary for firefighters across Canada is $166,000 . The average annual salary for a firefighter in Calgary, AB is $187,000 . This is 12% above the national average. The average annual salary for a firefighter in Edmonton, AB is $172,000 . This is approximately the same as the national average. Job Overview If you’re seeking a fast-paced career that will keep you active and doesn’t involve sitting behind a desk, becoming a firefighter could be a natural fit. Besides extinguishing fires and maintaining firefighting equipment, firefighters also respond to a wide range of other emergencies and provide emergency medical care. Credentials The credentials required for becoming a firefighter vary across the country. In order to apply for a position as a firefighter in Calgary or Edmonton , you must: Be at least 18 years of age Have a high school diploma Be physically fit Be eligible to work in Canada Possess a valid driver’s licence You must also complete further first aid and driving certifications prior to being hired. Job Outlook The job outlook for firefighters in the period between 2025-2026 is projected to be moderate in both Calgary and Edmonton. 2. Air Traffic Controller The average annual base salary for air traffic controllers across Canada is $100,000 . The average annual base salary for an air traffic controller in Calgary, AB is $92,000 . This is 8% below the national average. The average annual base salary for an air traffic controller in Edmonton, AB is $136,000 . This is 33% above the national average. Job Overview Air traffic controllers are responsible for managing the movement of aircraft within a designated airspace. They communicate with pilots, coordinate traffic flow management, monitor aircraft, and collaborate with other controllers to ensure a smooth and safe experience from takeoff to landing. Credentials In order to apply to become an air traffic controller in Canada, you must: Be 18 years old Have a high school diploma Be eligible to work in Canada You will also be required to pass NAV CANADA’s rigorous selection process, which involves numerous aptitude tests, assessments, and interviews. Job Outlook The job outlook for air traffic controllers in the period between 2025-2026 is projected to be moderate in Calgary and good in Edmonton. 3. Miner The average annual base salary for miners across Canada is $149,000 . The average annual base salary for a miner in Calgary, AB is $122,000 . This is 18% below the national average. The average annual base salary for a miner in Edmonton, AB is $99,000 . This is 34% below the national average. Job Overview Miners work underground in mines to drill, blast, and excavate ore. They are often responsible for the operation of heavy machinery and may be involved in tasks including loading and transporting ore, building shafts and/or installing elements such as lighting and pumps. Credentials The credentials for becoming a miner may include: Being certified in the basic Underground Hard Rock Miner Common Core program First aid certification Formal on-the-job training Job Outlook The job outlook for miners in the period between 2025-2026 is projected to be good in both Calgary and Edmonton. 4. Police Officer The average annual salary for police officers across Canada is $91,000 . The average annual salary for a police officer in Calgary, AB is $94,000 . This is approximately the same as the national average. The average annual salary for a police officer in Edmonton, AB is $86,000 . This is approximately the same as the national average. Job Overview Police officers keep citizens safe by responding to emergencies and actively patrolling neighbourhoods and communities. Other duties may include enforcing laws, investigating crimes, and building positive community relations. Credentials Specific credentials required to become a police officer vary across the country. Generally speaking, you must: Have a Canadian high school diploma Be physically fit and in good health Have a clean criminal record You will also need to be accepted into and complete a police academy training program. Job Outlook The job outlook for police officers in the period between 2025-2026 is projected to be good in both Calgary and Edmonton. 5. Construction Manager The average annual base salary for construction managers across Canada is $109,000 . The average annual base salary for a construction manager in Calgary, AB is $116,000 . This is 6% above the national average. The average annual base salary for a construction manager in Edmonton, AB is $109,000 . This is approximately the same as the national average. Job Overview A construction manager is responsible for overseeing construction projects from start to finish—from the planning phase right through to scheduling, hiring subcontractors, and completing the project on time and within budget. Credentials The credentials required for becoming a construction manager may include: A college diploma in construction technology and/or Extensive experience in the construction industry Job Outlook The job outlook for construction managers in the period between 2025-2026 is projected to be good in both Calgary and Edmonton. 6. Real Estate Agent The average annual base salary for real estate agents across Canada is $128,000 . The average annual base salary for a real estate agent in Calgary, AB is $116,000 . This is 9% below the national average. The average annual base salary for a real estate agent in Edmonton, AB is $73,000 . This is 43% below the national average. Job Overview Real estate agents work directly with clients to help them buy, sell, and/or lease properties. Their primary responsibilities include showing properties, determining the market value of properties, marketing properties, and mediating between buyers and sellers to achieve the best possible outcomes for their clients. Credentials The specific credentials required for real estate agents vary somewhat from province to province, but typically include: Being a minimum of 18 years old Having a high school diploma Being eligible to work in Canada Having a clean criminal record Passing the real estate licensing exam for your province (in Alberta, the pre-licensing education and licensing process take approximately 4 to 7 months) Job Outlook The job outlook for real estate agents in the period between 2025-2026 is projected to be good in both Calgary and Edmonton. 7. Maintenance Manager The average annual base salary for maintenance managers across Canada is $87,000 . The average annual base salary for a maintenance manager in Calgary, AB is $74,000 . This is 15% below the national average. The average annual base salary for a maintenance manager in Edmonton, AB is $89,000 . This is approximately the same as the national average. Job Overview A maintenance manager is responsible for overseeing the maintenance of a building or facility. This may include performing and/or scheduling routine inspections and maintenance, dealing with emergency break-downs, managing a budget, liaising with vendors, and maintaining organized maintenance records. Credentials The credentials required to become a maintenance manager in Canada can vary widely depending on the scope of the role. They may include: A high school diploma Maintenance and/or management experience Certifications and/or a diploma related to building maintenance Job Outlook The job outlook for maintenance managers in the period between 2025-2026 is projected to be moderate in both Calgary and Edmonton. 8. Airline Pilot The average annual base salary for airline pilots across Canada is $88,000 . The average annual base salary for an airline pilot in Calgary, AB is $117,000 . This is 33% above the national average. The average annual base salary for an airline pilot in Edmonton, AB is $53,000 . This is 40% below the national average. Job Overview Airline pilots safely transport passengers and cargo in commercial aircraft. Besides actively operating planes in-flight, they are also responsible for performing pre-flight checks, planning flight routes, communicating with air traffic control, and handling emergency situations that may unexpectedly arise. Credentials To be hired as a professional airline pilot in Canada, you must: Be at least 18 years old Earn a Canadian commercial pilot licence (includes a minimum of 80 hours of ground school instruction and 200 hours of flight time) Hold a current Category 1 Medical Certificate Job Outlook The job outlook for airline pilots in the period between 2025-2026 is projected to be moderate in Calgary and good in Edmonton. 9. Sales Manager The average annual base salary for sales managers across Canada is $82,000 . The average annual base salary for a sales manager in Calgary, AB is $84,000 . This is approximately the same as the national average. The average annual base salary for a sales manager in Edmonton, AB is $91,000 . This is 11% above the national average. Job Overview A sales manager oversees a team of salespeople to increase sales and revenue for a company or organization. Key duties include developing sales strategies, managing employees, and pinpointing areas for growth. Explore other top-paying jobs in business administration for more examples of lucrative careers that can be jump-started with a business administration diploma from Robertson College. Credentials The credentials required to become a sales manager in Canada can vary widely depending on the details of the role. They typically include: A diploma or other certification in business administration Relevant sales and management experience Job Outlook The job outlook for sales managers in the period between 2025-2026 is projected to be moderate in Calgary and limited in Edmonton. 10. Operations Manager The average annual base salary for operations managers across Canada is $81,000 . The average annual base salary for an operations manager in Calgary, AB is $74,000 . This is 9% below the national average. The average annual base salary for an operations manager in Edmonton, AB is $82,000 . This is approximately the same as the national average. Job Overview An operations manager oversees the day-to-day operations of a business or organization. Duties include optimizing efficiency, managing budgets, putting processes in place for quality control, managing teams, and much more. Interested in moving into an operations management role? Learn more about how upskilling can help your career* (*link to How Upskilling Can Help Your Career blog post once published) Credentials The following credentials are generally required to become an operations manager in Canada: A post-secondary certification in business administration Experience in project management, human resources, supply chain management, and/or accounting Job Outlook The job outlook for operations managers in the period between 2025-2026 is projected to be moderate in both Calgary and Edmonton. 11. Electrician The average annual base salary for electricians across Canada is $82,000 . The average annual base salary for an electrician in Calgary, AB is $73,000 . This is 10% below the national average. The average annual base salary for an electrician in Edmonton, AB is $75,000 . This is 7% below the national average. Job Overview Electricians are responsible for the installation, maintenance, and repair of electrical systems in residential, commercial, and industrial settings. Credentials Becoming an electrician in Canada requires the following: A high school diploma Completion of a 4 to 5-year apprenticeship program (this often includes formal post-secondary training) Earn a provincial journeyperson certificate (many electricians also apply for Red Seal certification, which is recognized interprovincially) Job Outlook The job outlook for electricians in the period between 2025-2026 is projected to be good in Calgary and moderate in Edmonton. 12. Executive Assistant The average annual base salary for executive assistants across Canada is $63,000 . The average annual base salary for an executive assistant in Calgary, AB is $62,000 . This is approximately the same as the national average. The average annual base salary for an executive assistant in Edmonton, AB is $62,000 . This is approximately the same as the national average. Job Overview An executive assistant performs high-level administrative tasks for a senior executive, such as a CEO. Responsibilities can include calendar management, travel booking, appointment coordination, document preparation, communications management, and much more. Explore Robertson College’s diploma programs to discover more careers in business administration in Calgary and Edmonton. Credentials In order to become an executive assistant in Canada, the following credentials are recommended: A post-secondary certification in business administration Experience as an administrative assistant Certifications in relevant computer systems Job Outlook The job outlook for executive assistants in the period between 2025-2026 is projected to be moderate in Calgary and good in Edmonton. Start Levelling Up Your Career Today With Robertson College A wide range of well-paying job opportunities are within reach for graduates of Robertson College’s streamlined diploma programs, including high-paying jobs for international students. To learn more about the admission requirements for specific programs and kickstart a fulfilling and financially rewarding career without a university degree, contact one of our Student Advisors today ! We look forward to meeting you. Matthew Lau Matthew Lau is a marketing expert with over 10 years of experience, specializing in integrated marketing, project management, and strategic planning. He has significantly enhanced digital engagement and conversion rates across the education, retail, and media industries. Matthew has also provided marketing consultancy services, assisting businesses in developing and implementing effective digital marketing and eCommerce strategies, creating compelling content, optimizing their online presence, and achieving substantial growth. The Complete Guide For Business Professionals What Is Project Management? The Complete Guide For Business Professionals By Matthew Lau Matthew Lau is a marketing expert with over 10 years of experience, specializing in integrated marketing, project management, and strategic planning. He has significantly enhanced digital engagement and conversion rates across the education, retail, and media industries. Matthew has also provided marketing consultancy services, assisting businesses in developing and implementing effective digital marketing and eCommerce strategies, creating compelling content, optimizing their online presence, and achieving substantial growth. May 8, 2025 15 min SHARE quick answer Project management is about managing the project life cycle—seeing a project through from its conceptualization to its completion. Project management professionals oversee teams and processes to create better outcomes for everyone. In This Article Key Takeaways What Is Project Management? Who Uses Project Management? Project Management Types Project Management Phases Popular Project Management Tools What Makes A Good Project Plan? Conclusion In this article, you’ll learn what project management is, what project managers do, which businesses need project management, the types of project management that are utilized, the phases of project management, and more. Want to have a positive impact on a business, the team members working in that business, and the customers and stakeholders who rely on that business? A career in project management might be perfect for you. At Robertson College, we offer a number of courses that can lead to careers in project management at our college in Calgary . Key Takeaways Project management is the process of seeing a project through from its conceptualization to its completion. Project managers set goals, define timelines, manage resources, implement quality controls and success metrics, manage risk, and facilitate communication. Project management is used by companies across all industries. There are several different styles of project management, including agile, waterfall, and lean. Project management can be split into five phases: Initiation, planning, execution, monitoring, and closing. Project managers use a variety of different tools to keep projects running smoothly. Robertson College can help you start a new career in project management. What Is Project Management? What’s Considered A Project? A project is an effort undertaken to accomplish a set goal. That’s a fairly vague description, but it’s vague for a reason: A project can be almost anything, from a simple effort to streamline scheduling at a call centre to a tremendous undertaking like creating a federal government budget. All projects, no matter how big or small, share some common traits. These traits include: Temporary: Projects always have a defined beginning and end. This makes project management different from general management, where there are no set endpoints. Defined goals: Projects aim to accomplish something —there is always a clear objective in mind. The clearer the objective, the more well-defined the project may be. Constraints: Projects are almost always tied to limits: Time, budget, resources, and scope. Too many constraints and a project may not be feasible; too few, and the project loses definition and can become wasteful. Team effort: Projects must be carefully planned and coordinated; a project is typically led by a single project manager who outlines the goals, coordinates between team members, and communicates with stakeholders. Large projects are often broken down into smaller projects, known as subprojects. This process is known as nesting. Nested projects typically have several project managers, all reporting to a senior project manager. Core Components Of Project Management Project management is all about achieving the desired results of a project while working within set constraints. Project managers coordinate project team members and resources to accomplish their goals. This is complex work, and project managers must consider all of the core components of a project to steer it successfully. These components include: Setting goals: Goals, also called project objectives, must be measurable. They can be outputs, like developing a new product, outcomes, like new customers purchasing that product, and benefits, like increased market share . Broad strategic objectives, like growing the customer base of a business by 30% in 2 years, are broken down into smaller goals, which are then assigned their own projects. Time-management: Projects should always be time-bound—this makes it easier to estimate how many resources the project will require, and helps managers and stakeholders coordinate in order to ensure that related projects are finished at or near the same time. You wouldn’t, for example, want to bring a product to market without the marketing team being ready to advertise—and the marketing team wouldn’t want to advertise a product that doesn’t exist. Project managers keep everyone on task, report delays, and work to get the project completed on time. Resource management: Resources is a broad term for all of the inputs that a project needs to reach its goal. The most common input is money—to pay staff, pay contractors, purchase equipment, and more. Staff and equipment are resources in their own right, too—a senior engineer may only have so much time to allocate to a project, so their time must be used carefully. A project manager must carefully budget and allocate resources to accomplish the project’s goals on time. Risk management: If a project fails, a tremendous amount of time and money can be lost. Project managers must be able to predict risk, place safeguards to mitigate risk, accept risk, and push forward where necessary. They must also be able to communicate the need to accept risk to stakeholders. Quality control: One of the biggest risks to a project is low quality control; if the outputs and outcomes of a project fall short of what’s desired, the project may be considered a failure. A project manager must create quality requirements, and implement quality assurance measures and controls. Communication: No project exists in a vacuum; project managers must constantly communicate with project team members and stakeholders, updating them on the status of the project and keeping them informed about any relevant changes. Why We Use Project Management Companies use project management techniques and hire project managers because certain projects are incredibly complex. Project management is used when: Projects are projected to have an outsized impact on the business Projects are projected to be particularly complex or novel Risk management and change management are particularly important By investing in project management for important and complex projects, businesses have a better chance of achieving the results they are aiming for, all while staying within scope and completing the project on time and on budget. Who Uses Project Management? Project management is used across all industries. Some of the industries that use project management most often include: Tech and IT Manufacturing Engineering Finance Healthcare and Pharmaceuticals Construction Law Energy Thanks to their high demand across several industries, project managers in Canada have excellent job prospects; some project manager positions pay a median wage of $45.67/hour or about $90,000 yearly. Project Management Types There are several different styles of project management, each with its own advantages and disadvantages. Project managers will typically take courses in one or more of these project management styles. At Robertson College, we offer courses that include deep dives into project management, including comprehensive reviews of the PMBOK Guide written by the Project Management Institute. This guide includes an overview of agile, one of the project management styles we will explore below. Projects all have their own shape, and a project manager is not obligated to adhere strictly to one style or type of project management; some flexibility and broad knowledge can help improve outcomes. Below, we’ve reviewed six project management types to give you an idea of the pros and cons of different approaches: Agile Agile project management is a broad term for an iterative style of management. Agile management is governed by four basic values: Individuals and interactions over processes and tools Working software (or other outputs) over comprehensive documentation Customer collaboration over contract negotiation Responding to change by following a plan The elements on the right-hand side aren’t irrelevant—great tools, processes, documentation, negotiation, and planning are all important—but they should, in an agile framework, be secondary to the elements on the left. When applying an agile framework, your goal is to stay flexible. You will guide your team into short sprints—usually one to four weeks—in which you move toward a milestone in your project. You might develop a piece of software or prototype a product. You will then share that software or product with stakeholders or customers, see what they think, and then iterate. Cross-functional teams are common; software developers, marketers, UX testers, and other team members, all working together. This approach allows you to navigate uncertainty, changing demands, and unexpected roadblocks by staying adaptable. Agile frameworks are commonly used in: Software development Product development Marketing and design Biotech/Medtech Agile works best on projects that are not constrained by tight regulations. Civil engineering firms, for example, cannot iterate on a bridge, find out it collapses, and then try again—they must create a detailed plan from the start of their project. Waterfall Waterfall can be thought of as the opposite approach to agile; it is a top-down style of project management where everything is planned carefully from the start. In a waterfall project, there are five classic phases: Requirements: In this phase, you identify, describe, develop, and acquire everything you need: Resources, timeframes, quality metrics, costs, risks, and more. Design: In this phase, you plan how the project will be completed; the schedule, the budget, and more. You will lead teams that design objects or processes; this may include architecture, software design, UX/UI design, and more. Implementation: In this phase, you execute the plan developed in the last phase; you may code a program, construct a house, or manufacture a product. Verification: Once the project is nearing completion, you test the output or outcome, get feedback, and fix anything that can be fixed. Maintenance: When the project is complete, you may need to support end-users, update the product, and receive feedback for future products. Though you may not manage the maintenance phase yourself, as a project manager, considering how ongoing maintenance can be implemented at the end of your project can lead to a smoother transition. Waterfall project management is far less flexible than agile methods, but that lack of flexibility lends this style of management structure. Regulatory agencies, investors, and other stakeholders can look at carefully crafted waterfall plans and approve or fund them more easily, as every step of the project is laid out upfront. Lean Lean project management is all about reducing waste and maximizing value. Efficiency and simplicity are the hallmarks of this style. When running a lean project, you might: Visualize value streams: Value streams are all of the actions required to produce an output or outcome. In a lean project, you will create value stream maps to understand where value is added, and what non-value-adding activities exist in the project. Eliminating waste: Lean is primarily derived from the management philosophies of Toyota. Three Japanese words describe the different types of waste: Muda (waste through processes), Muri (waste through overwork), and Mura (waste through uneven workloads). As a lean project manager, you will be tasked with reducing or eliminating all three types of waste. Pulling work: Work should only be done when needed. Your marketing team should not develop marketing materials before a product is finished. You should manufacture products to meet demand, rather than holding onto inventory that may not sell. Lean also focuses on continuous improvement (often referred to using the Japanese word kaizen) and empowering people by ensuring that management actively works with and engages with every team involved in the project. Kanban Kanban is a project management process that is closely linked to both lean (from which it originated) and agile (which adopted the practice). The goal of kanban is to reduce bottlenecks by balancing demand with capacity. Visualizations are a key component of kanban. The most common form of visualization is the kanban board, which at its most basic form consists of three columns: To-do, in progress, and done. Here is a simple visualization: To-do In Progress Done Task A Task D Task B Task E   Task C Project managers may use a number of different tools, like Trello or Jira, or physical tools like Post-it Notes to create kanban boards. There are controls placed on when a project can move from to-do to in-progress, and from in-progress to done. Typically, a team or team member is only allowed to work on a certain number of tasks at a time, and the total number of tasks in progress may be limited. Project managers also usually put quality control measures in place, so a task must reach a certain quality threshold before it can be considered done. As part of a lean philosophy, kanban helps reduce two forms of waste: Overwork and uneven work distribution. Six Sigma Six Sigma is a data-driven project management methodology that focuses on reducing defects and variations in processes and products. The goal of Six Sigma is to create processes that only produce 3.4 defects per million outputs. The tool used is a detailed analysis, including: Root cause analysis Statistical analysis Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA) Six Sigma is most frequently used in projects where quality control is paramount, including: Manufacturing in tightly regulated industries Healthcare Logistics The goal of Six Sigma is repeatability; it works beautifully with lean and waterfall project management but may be less suited to agile project management styles. Scrum Scrum is a project management framework typically used in agile projects. In this framework, individuals are split into scrum teams, with each team consisting of: A product owner , who represents and communicates with product stakeholders, including customers, committees, and teams outside the scrum Developers or team members , who develop and create the output in question The scrum master is a project manager who oversees the scrum team and helps plan, problem-solve, communicate, and coach. Scrum timelines are divided into sprints, where each team member works on a specific goal—sprints typically vary in time from one week to one month. Every day, there is a daily scrum meeting where team members speak for 15 minutes or less about progress and roadblocks. Additionally, at the end of a sprint, two post-sprint events are held; one external, with stakeholders, and one internal, to improve future scrums. Project Management Phases Project management can be split into five phases: Initiation, planning, execution, monitoring, and closing. Initiation Phase In the initiation phase, you define the overall goal of your project and secure buy-in from stakeholders. The initiation phase can be very involved—in waterfall projects, initiation comes after detailed planning. In other project management approaches like agile, initiation can be shorter. Here are some steps you might take in the initiation phase: Create a business case Pitch your project to key stakeholders Run a feasibility study Assemble your team and resources Planning Phase The planning phase is all about outlining project goals, budgets, controls, metrics, timelines, and more. Depending on your chosen project management style, this phase can outline plans that will take weeks (in agile frameworks), years (in long-term waterfall frameworks), and anything in between. The detail and scope of your budget, resources, metrics, and more will also vary depending on the framework and the project. Some steps you might take in the planning phase include: Defining your goals and milestones Creating success metrics and quality controls Assigning roles and responsibilities Setting up your budget Outlining your schedule Creating a communication plan Execution Phase In the execution phase, you implement the plan you have created. Your role as a project manager will be to support your team, answer questions they may have, oversee the project, and facilitate communication. Teams may need new resources. You may need to help resolve conflicts. You may need to coach team members. In this phase, everything is moving; your role is to keep things running as smoothly as possible. Monitoring Phase The monitoring phase happens at the same time as the execution phase. While team members are working on the project, project managers are monitoring their progress, and comparing performance to the success metrics and quality controls they set in place. Monitoring involves: Tracking hours Monitoring the project budget Preventing scope creep Performing quality checks Updating risk assessment Communicating with stakeholders and managing expectations Closing Phase The closing phase occurs when the project is completed. Vendors are paid, products are delivered, contracts are signed, and the project is brought to an end. During the closing phase, project managers should conduct an honest assessment of the project: What went well, what didn’t, and what can be improved next time. There is something new to learn in every project. Meeting with team members and stakeholders once a project is completed will lead to greater success in your next project! Popular Project Management Tools Effective project management relies on tools to communicate, improve workflow, manage documents, monitor time and resource usage, and reduce risk—among other things. Here are a few examples of the tools that project managers may use: Project Management Software Trello Asana Jira Microsoft Project Communication Software Slack Microsoft Teams Zoom Google Meet Document Management Software Google Workspace Microsoft OneDrive Dropbox Time-Tracking Software Clockify Harvest Hubstaff Risk Management Tools Risk registers RiskyProject LogicManager What Makes A Good Project Plan? A good project plan features: Clear goals A well-defined scope A project budget Detailed and realistic timelines Actionable tasks Resource allocation A communication plan Risk management Quality controls Success metrics Writing a project plan can be daunting if you don’t know how. We’re here to help. In business courses like our Logistics and Supply Chain Management course, you will learn how to build a project plan from the ground up. Conclusion Project management is an exciting, high-demand, constantly changing field. Every project is different—new people, new goals, new budgets, and new ways to learn and grow. If you’re looking for a dynamic career that helps people and businesses, project management could be the perfect choice. At Robertson College, we teach business administration in Calgary ; this course, or one of our other business courses, could be the first step toward a fulfilling career in project management. Learn more by connecting with one of our Student Advisors today . They will personally provide support and guidance, answering any questions you might have about our programs. Matthew Lau Matthew Lau is a marketing expert with over 10 years of experience, specializing in integrated marketing, project management, and strategic planning. He has significantly enhanced digital engagement and conversion rates across the education, retail, and media industries. Matthew has also provided marketing consultancy services, assisting businesses in developing and implementing effective digital marketing and eCommerce strategies, creating compelling content, optimizing their online presence, and achieving substantial growth.
